items.prompt_md: Why is a BST's ordering property described as 'global' rather than 'local'?
options: Because every node must be bounded by ALL of its ancestors' constraints, not just compared against its immediate parent or children
Because the tree must be perfectly balanced across its entire height
Because every node must store a pointer back to the root
Because the values must span a contiguous range of integers
items.answer: Because every node must be bounded by ALL of its ancestors' constraints, not just compared against its immediate parent or children
items.explanation_md: A node's left subtree must be less than it and its right subtree greater, transitively, all the way down — a locally-sorted parent-child pair can still violate a constraint from a grandparent or higher.

items.prompt_md: In `[5, 4, 6, null, null, 3, 7]`, why does a check that only compares each node to its immediate children fail to catch that this isn't a valid BST?
options: Node 3 passes its local comparison against parent 6 (3 < 6), but is never compared against the root 5, whose right subtree it illegally sits in
The tree is missing a null child somewhere, which the local check can't detect
The local check runs in O(n^2) time, so it times out before reaching node 3
Node 3 and node 7 have the same parent, which local checks always misclassify
items.answer: Node 3 passes its local comparison against parent 6 (3 < 6), but is never compared against the root 5, whose right subtree it illegally sits in
items.explanation_md: 3 sits in the root's right subtree, where everything must exceed 5 — but a local check never compares 3 against 5, only against its immediate parent 6.

items.prompt_md: What do the `low` and `high` bounds represent as they're threaded through a recursive BST validation?
options: The shrinking valid range a node's value must fall inside, inherited from every ancestor decision made so far
The minimum and maximum depth the recursion is allowed to reach
The smallest and largest node values seen anywhere in the tree so far
The left and right child pointers of the current node
items.answer: The shrinking valid range a node's value must fall inside, inherited from every ancestor decision made so far
items.explanation_md: Each recursive call narrows `(low, high)`: going left tightens the upper bound to the parent's value, going right tightens the lower bound — encoding every ancestor constraint without re-checking them explicitly.

items.prompt_md: When recursing into a node's right child during BST validation, how do the bounds change?
options: `low` becomes the current node's value; `high` stays whatever was inherited from above
`high` becomes the current node's value; `low` stays whatever was inherited from above
Both `low` and `high` reset to negative and positive infinity
Both bounds are discarded since the right subtree only needs a local comparison
items.answer: `low` becomes the current node's value; `high` stays whatever was inherited from above
items.explanation_md: Everything in a right subtree must exceed the current node, so the current value becomes the new lower bound while the inherited upper bound is passed through unchanged.

items.prompt_md: Why does an in-order traversal of a valid BST visit nodes in ascending sorted order, with no extra sorting step?
options: Because in-order visits left subtree, then node, then right subtree — and the BST invariant guarantees everything in the left subtree is smaller and everything in the right subtree is larger
Because in-order traversal always processes the smallest-depth nodes first
Because BSTs store an auxiliary sorted array alongside the tree structure
Because in-order traversal visits the root first, which is always the median value
items.answer: Because in-order visits left subtree, then node, then right subtree — and the BST invariant guarantees everything in the left subtree is smaller and everything in the right subtree is larger
items.explanation_md: The traversal order (left, node, right) lines up exactly with the BST's ordering invariant (smaller, this value, larger), so sorted output falls out for free.

items.prompt_md: How does 'find the kth smallest value in a BST' reduce to a traversal problem?
options: Walk the tree in-order and stop as soon as you've visited k values — the kth one visited is the answer
Walk the tree in pre-order and take the kth value pushed onto the call stack
Sort all node values into an array first, since in-order traversal alone can't guarantee sorted order
Do a BFS and take the kth node dequeued
items.answer: Walk the tree in-order and stop as soon as you've visited k values — the kth one visited is the answer
items.explanation_md: In-order traversal of a BST already produces ascending order, so the kth smallest is simply the kth value the traversal reaches; no separate sort is needed.

items.prompt_md: What's a common bug when validating BSTs with bound comparisons?
options: Using `<=`/`>=` instead of strict `<`/`>`, which silently accepts trees with duplicate values in the wrong place
Forgetting to visit the root node at all
Recursing into the left child before checking the current node's bounds
Using integers instead of floats for the node values
items.answer: Using `<=`/`>=` instead of strict `<`/`>`, which silently accepts trees with duplicate values in the wrong place
items.explanation_md: Most BST definitions treat duplicates as invalid unless explicitly allowed; sloppy inequality direction lets a duplicate slip past the bound check undetected.

items.prompt_md: Why is `float('-inf')`/`float('inf')` a reasonable choice for the initial bounds at the root call?
options: Because the root has no ancestor constraints yet, so any value should be accepted at that point
Because it forces the algorithm to reject any tree whose root isn't zero
Because floating point comparisons are faster than integer comparisons
Because it matches the maximum recursion depth allowed by the language
items.answer: Because the root has no ancestor constraints yet, so any value should be accepted at that point
items.explanation_md: The root hasn't inherited any restriction from an ancestor, so its valid range is unbounded in both directions until its own children narrow it.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of validating a BST with the range-propagation approach, on an n-node tree of height h?
options: O(n) time (each node visited once), O(h) space (the recursion call stack)
O(n log n) time, O(n) space
O(h) time, O(h) space
O(n) time, O(1) space
items.answer: O(n) time (each node visited once), O(h) space (the recursion call stack)
items.explanation_md: Every node is visited exactly once regardless of the bounds threaded through, so it's O(n) time; the only extra memory is the recursion stack, bounded by the tree's height h.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of finding the kth smallest element via in-order traversal, and does it change with a balanced vs. skewed tree?
options: O(k) time in the best case since traversal can stop early, up to O(n) worst case if k is close to n — this is unaffected by whether the tree is balanced
O(log n) time always, because BST lookups are always logarithmic
O(n^2) time regardless of k, since every node must be compared to every other node
O(h) time always, since only one path down the tree needs to be visited
items.answer: O(k) time in the best case since traversal can stop early, up to O(n) worst case if k is close to n — this is unaffected by whether the tree is balanced
items.explanation_md: In-order traversal can stop as soon as it has emitted k values, so the work done is proportional to k, not to the tree's height or balance — balance affects lookup by value, not this positional traversal.

items.prompt_md: Why is a BST's O(h) search bound not automatically O(log n)?
options: Because h depends on how the tree was built — an unbalanced BST built from sorted input can degenerate into a linked list where h = n
Because search always has to check every node exactly once
Because O(h) and O(log n) are always mathematically identical for any binary tree
Because floating-point bound comparisons add an extra logarithmic factor
items.answer: Because h depends on how the tree was built — an unbalanced BST built from sorted input can degenerate into a linked list where h = n
items.explanation_md: O(log n) only holds when the tree is reasonably balanced; a BST built by inserting already-sorted values produces a chain-shaped tree of height n, making search O(n).

items.prompt_md: You're given a BST and a target value, and asked to check whether the value exists anywhere in the tree. What's the best approach?
options: Use the BST ordering to go left or right at each node, pruning to a single path toward the target
DFS the whole tree comparing every node to the target
BFS level by level, checking every node as it's dequeued
Build a hash set of all values first, then do a membership check
items.answer: Use the BST ordering to go left or right at each node, pruning to a single path toward the target
items.explanation_md: The ordering invariant means the target can only be in one direction at each node, so search prunes to a single root-to-leaf path in O(h) instead of visiting every node.

items.prompt_md: You must insert a new value into a BST while preserving its ordering invariant. Which approach fits?
options: Follow the BST ordering down from the root until you find the null slot where the value belongs, then attach a new node there
Rebuild the entire tree from scratch with the new value included
BFS to find the shallowest empty slot in the tree, regardless of value order
Insert the value into a separate sorted array alongside the tree
items.answer: Follow the BST ordering down from the root until you find the null slot where the value belongs, then attach a new node there
items.explanation_md: Just like search, insertion follows the ordering invariant down a single path — the correct spot is wherever that path first hits a null child.

items.prompt_md: You need to determine whether a given binary tree satisfies the BST ordering property everywhere, not just at each parent-child pair. What approach is correct?
options: DFS the whole tree, threading a shrinking (low, high) range through the recursion for each node
Only compare each node to its immediate left and right children
BFS level by level, checking that each level's values are sorted left to right
Sort all the node values and check the sorted list equals the in-order traversal, without ever comparing parents to children
items.answer: DFS the whole tree, threading a shrinking (low, high) range through the recursion for each node
items.explanation_md: Full BST validation is a global property — a local parent-child comparison misses violations from further-up ancestors, so bounds must be carried down the whole DFS.

items.prompt_md: You need the 3rd smallest value stored in a BST. What's the best approach?
options: In-order traversal, stopping once you've visited 3 values
BFS by level, taking the 3rd node dequeued
Pre-order traversal, taking the 3rd node visited
Hash every value into a set, then sort the set and take the 3rd element
items.answer: In-order traversal, stopping once you've visited 3 values
items.explanation_md: In-order traversal of a BST yields values in ascending order for free, so the kth smallest is just the kth value the traversal reaches — no separate sort needed.

items.prompt_md: You're given a plain (non-BST) binary tree and asked whether a target value exists anywhere in it. What approach fits, and why is it different from the BST case?
options: DFS the whole tree comparing every node to the target, since there's no ordering invariant to prune the search
Use the ordering invariant to prune to one path, exactly as with a BST
Binary search over the tree's array representation
Two pointers starting from the leftmost and rightmost leaves
items.answer: DFS the whole tree comparing every node to the target, since there's no ordering invariant to prune the search
items.explanation_md: Without a BST's ordering guarantee, there's no way to know which side to prune, so every node must be visited — a full DFS or BFS, not a pruned path search.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether two given arrays contain exactly the same multiset of values, in any order. What's the best approach?
options: Build a hash map of value-to-count for each array and compare the maps
Use BST ordering to prune the search to one path
Do a DFS over a tree structure comparing nodes pairwise
BFS level by level over the arrays
items.answer: Build a hash map of value-to-count for each array and compare the maps
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: there's no tree here at all, just two flat collections — counting frequencies with a hash map is the right tool, not any tree traversal.

items.prompt_md: You need to reverse a singly linked list in place. What's the best approach?
options: Walk the list with prev/curr pointers, flipping each node's `next` pointer as you go
Use BST ordering to prune the traversal
In-order traversal collecting values into a sorted list
BFS by level, treating each node as if it had children
items.answer: Walk the list with prev/curr pointers, flipping each node's `next` pointer as you go
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: a singly linked list has no branching or ordering invariant to exploit — it's a linked-list pointer-rewiring problem, not a tree problem.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether a string of parentheses is balanced. What's the best approach?
options: Push opening brackets onto a stack and pop-and-match on each closing bracket
Treat the string as a BST and validate its ordering
In-order traversal of the string's characters
BFS over the string's characters level by level
items.answer: Push opening brackets onto a stack and pop-and-match on each closing bracket
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: bracket matching is a classic stack problem (LIFO nesting), unrelated to any tree ordering invariant.

items.prompt_md: You need to validate that a binary tree is a valid BST, but you're told the tree can be extremely tall and skewed. Does the (low, high) bound approach still work, and at what cost?
options: Yes — the algorithm is unchanged; it's still O(n) time, but O(h) recursion space grows to O(n) in the skewed worst case
No — bounds only work correctly on balanced trees
Yes, and the recursion space stays O(log n) regardless of skew
No — a skewed tree requires switching to a hash-map-based validation
items.answer: Yes — the algorithm is unchanged; it's still O(n) time, but O(h) recursion space grows to O(n) in the skewed worst case
items.explanation_md: The bound-propagation logic doesn't care about shape, but its space cost is tied to height h, which equals n for a fully skewed tree — still correct, just worse on space.

items.prompt_md: You need to find the smallest value stored anywhere in a BST. What's the best approach?
options: Follow left children from the root until reaching a node with no left child
In-order traversal, taking the last value visited
BFS by level, taking the first node dequeued
DFS the whole tree comparing every node's value to a running minimum
items.answer: Follow left children from the root until reaching a node with no left child
items.explanation_md: The BST ordering guarantees the minimum is always the leftmost node, so a single downward path (O(h)) finds it — no need to visit every node or traverse in full.
